title: Getting started with daos
text: f() alias glue::glue(). interpolates R expressions embedded {} inside string.
code: year <- 2026 f("Report for {year}") #> Report for 2026 f("1 + 1 = {1 + 1}") #> 1 + 1 = 2

title: Getting started with daos
text: nowf() returns current time formatted string. Useful building timestamped file names. Combine two quick timestamped path:
code: nowf()                  # default: YYYYMMDD #> [1] "20260520" nowf("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")  # custom format #> [1] "2026-05-20 22:22" f("log/{nowf()}/pipeline.log")

title: Getting started with daos
code: quiet(message("this will not appear")) quiet(warning("neither will this"))  # Useful when loading packages without the startup banner: # quiet(library(tidyverse))

title: Getting started with daos
text: Unlike rlang::is_empty(), is_blank() treats empty strings -NA vectors blank.
code: is_blank(NULL)        # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(NA)          # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank("")          # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(c(NA, NA))   # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(0)           # FALSE #> [1] FALSE is_blank("text")      # FALSE #> [1] FALSE

title: Getting started with daos
text: Returns right-hand side whenever left-hand side blank (using logic is_blank()).
code: NULL %??% "default" #> [1] "default" NA   %??% 0 #> [1] 0 ""   %??% "unknown" #> [1] "unknown" 42   %??% 99          # returns 42 #> [1] 42

title: Getting started with daos
text: %like% wraps grepl() keeps NA values NA rather converting FALSE.
code: c("sedan", "SUV", NA, "truck") %like% "^S" #> [1] FALSE  TRUE    NA FALSE  # In a dplyr pipeline: filter(ggplot2::mpg, model %like% "\\d+") |>   count(model) #> # A tibble: 19 × 2 #>    model                      n #>    <chr>                  <int> #>  1 4runner 4wd                6 #>  2 a4                         7 #>  3 a4 quattro                 8 #>  4 a6 quattro                 3 #>  5 c1500 suburban 2wd         5 #>  6 caravan 2wd               11 #>  7 dakota pickup 4wd          9 #>  8 durango 4wd                7 #>  9 expedition 2wd             3 #> 10 explorer 4wd               6 #> 11 f150 pickup 4wd            7 #> 12 grand cherokee 4wd         8 #> 13 k1500 tahoe 4wd            4 #> 14 land cruiser wagon 4wd     2 #> 15 mountaineer 4wd            4 #> 16 navigator 2wd              3 #> 17 pathfinder 4wd             4 #> 18 ram 1500 pickup 4wd       10 #> 19 toyota tacoma 4wd          7

title: Getting started with daos
text: read_files() expands glue syntax path strings, aborts immediately file missing, reads files automatic format detection. single path returns object directly; multiple paths return named list progress bar. Supported formats: csv, tsv, parquet, feather, xlsx, xls, rds, sas7bdat, sav, por, xpt, dta, json, ndjson, jsonl, yaml, yml, txt. Supply custom reader override auto-detection add arguments:
code: # Single file (returns object directly): df <- read_files("data/results.parquet")  # Multiple files with glue expansion — returns a named list: lst <- read_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet", names = 0:9) read_files(   "data/dat{0:9}.parquet",   reader = \(x) arrow::read_parquet(x, col_select = 1:5) )

title: Getting started with daos
text: Set = "bind" row-bind files single tibble. source column records origin row. Column types always reconciled automatically.
code: df <- read_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet", names = 0:9, out = "bind")

title: Getting started with daos
text: Set = "unpack" assign file named variable calling environment:
code: read_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet", names = paste0("dat", 0:9), out = "unpack") # dat0, dat1, ..., dat9 are now in your environment

what: summon() — retrieve objects by name pattern
title: Getting started with daos
text: Collect objects whose names match regex back list:
code: dat1 <- data.frame(x = 1) dat2 <- data.frame(x = 2) dat3 <- data.frame(x = 3)  result <- summon("^dat\\d+$") names(result) #> [1] "dat1" "dat2" "dat3"

title: Getting started with daos
text: Greenlandic TA files:
code: df <- read_ta("ta.file")

what: view_types() — compare column types across data frames
title: Getting started with daos
text: view_types() shows pillar::type_sum() type column every dataset supplied. Invaluable joins binding read_files(= "bind").
code: df_a <- data.frame(x = 1L,  y = "a", z = TRUE) df_b <- data.frame(x = 1.0, y = "b", z = 1L)  # All columns: view_types(df_a, df_b) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   column df_a  df_b  #>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> #> 1 x      int   dbl   #> 2 y      chr   chr   #> 3 z      lgl   int # Only columns where types differ: view_types(df_a, df_b, diff = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   column df_a  df_b  #>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> #> 1 x      int   dbl   #> 2 z      lgl   int # Check that a specific column has the expected type: view_types(df_a, df_b, focus = c(x = "int")) #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>   column df_b  #>   <chr>  <chr> #> 1 x      dbl # Returns 0 rows if all match, otherwise shows the offending datasets

what: size_env() — object sizes in an environment
title: Getting started with daos
code: big   <- 1:1e6 small <- letters size_env()        # all objects, largest first #> # A tibble: 9 × 3 #>   name      size      pretty #>   <chr>    <dbl> <fs::bytes> #> 1 big    4000048       3.81M #> 2 result    2648       2.59K #> 3 small     1712       1.67K #> 4 df_a      1064       1.04K #> 5 df_b      1064       1.04K #> 6 dat1       736         736 #> 7 dat2       736         736 #> 8 dat3       736         736 #> 9 year        56          56 size_env(n = 2)   # top 2 only #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   name      size      pretty #>   <chr>    <dbl> <fs::bytes> #> 1 big    4000048       3.81M #> 2 result    2648       2.59K

what: expect_empty() — assert a data frame is empty
title: Getting started with daos
text: Use expect_empty() checkpoint pipeline. succeeds silently data frame rows, warns rows (unless abort_msg set, case aborts). log argument writes timestamped entry file — useful automated pipelines want minimal audit trail:
code: # Success: data.frame() |> expect_empty(success_msg = "All good — no rows") #> ✔ All good — no rows  # Warning: filter(ggplot2::mpg, cyl < 0) |>   expect_empty(warn_msg = "Unexpected: negative cylinder count") #> ✔ The dataset is empty. # Hard error: filter(dplyr::starwars, height < 0) |>   expect_empty(abort_msg = "Impossible: negative height") log_path <- f("log/{nowf()}/checks.log") checker  <- purrr::partial(expect_empty, log = log_path)  filter(dplyr::starwars, name == "Harry Potter") |>   checker(success_msg = "No fictional characters")  filter(dplyr::starwars, height > 250) |>   checker(warn_msg = "Unusually tall characters found")

what: flag_duplicates() — detect and label duplicate rows
title: Getting started with daos
text: flag_duplicates() prepends two columns: isdup (logical) dupid (integer group identifier, 0 unique rows). Combine expect_empty() pipeline validation gate:
code: flag_duplicates(ggplot2::mpg) #> # A tibble: 234 × 13 #>    isdup dupid manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy #>    <lgl> <int> <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> <int> <int> #>  1 FALSE     0 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto… f        18    29 #>  2 FALSE     0 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 #>  3 FALSE     0 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 #>  4 FALSE     0 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 #>  5 FALSE     0 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto… f        16    26 #>  6 FALSE     0 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manu… f        18    26 #>  7 FALSE     0 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 #>  8 FALSE     0 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manu… 4        18    26 #>  9 FALSE     0 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 #> 10 FALSE     0 audi         a4 quattro   2    2008     4 manu… 4        20    28 #> # ℹ 224 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: fl <chr>, class <chr> # Check specific columns: flag_duplicates(ggplot2::mpg, manufacturer, model, year) |>   filter(isdup) |>   arrange(dupid) #> # A tibble: 227 × 13 #>    isdup dupid manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy #>    <lgl> <int> <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> <int> <int> #>  1 TRUE      1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto… f        18    29 #>  2 TRUE      1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 #>  3 TRUE      1 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto… f        16    26 #>  4 TRUE      1 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manu… f        18    26 #>  5 TRUE      2 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 #>  6 TRUE      2 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 #>  7 TRUE      2 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 #>  8 TRUE      3 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manu… 4        18    26 #>  9 TRUE      3 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 #> 10 TRUE      3 audi         a4 quattro   2.8  1999     6 auto… 4        15    25 #> # ℹ 217 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: fl <chr>, class <chr> ggplot2::mpg |>   flag_duplicates() |>   filter(isdup) |>   expect_empty(warn_msg = "Duplicate rows detected") #> Warning: Duplicate rows detected

what: split_by() — split a data frame into a named list
title: Getting started with daos
text: split_by() named version dplyr::group_split(): Multiple grouping columns joined .sep:
code: parts <- split_by(ggplot2::mpg, manufacturer) names(parts)[1:5] #> [1] "audi"      "chevrolet" "dodge"     "ford"      "honda" head(parts[["audi"]]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 11 #>   manufacturer model displ  year   cyl trans      drv     cty   hwy fl    class  #>   <chr>        <chr> <dbl> <int> <int> <chr>      <chr> <int> <int> <chr> <chr>  #> 1 audi         a4      1.8  1999     4 auto(l5)   f        18    29 p     compa… #> 2 audi         a4      1.8  1999     4 manual(m5) f        21    29 p     compa… #> 3 audi         a4      2    2008     4 manual(m6) f        20    31 p     compa… #> 4 audi         a4      2    2008     4 auto(av)   f        21    30 p     compa… #> 5 audi         a4      2.8  1999     6 auto(l5)   f        16    26 p     compa… #> 6 audi         a4      2.8  1999     6 manual(m5) f        18    26 p     compa… parts2 <- split_by(ggplot2::mpg, cyl, drv, .sep = "-") names(parts2)[1:4] #> [1] "4-4" "4-f" "5-f" "6-4"

title: Getting started with daos
text: find_signs() uses meet---middle algorithm find sign assignment (+1, -1, 0) set values signed sum equals specified total. Useful importing accounting data sign conventions unknown. can pin known signs allow max_zeros items excluded:
code: items <- data.frame(   label = c("revenue", "costs", "tax", "total"),   value = c(1000, 400, 100, 500) )  find_signs(items, label, value, total_label = "total") #> # A tibble: 0 × 0 find_signs(   items,   label, value,   total_label = "total",   positive    = "revenue",   negative    = c("costs", "tax"),   max_zeros   = 1L )

what: cpr_info() — extract birth date, age, sex, and validity
title: Getting started with daos
text: cpr_info() appends one derived columns data frame using official CPR Register century-detection rules. Dashes spaces stripped automatically; nine-digit numbers zero-padded recover values lost leading zero Excel. Choose subset columns optionally rename : custom reference date shifts age calculation:

what: track_last_df() — auto-save the last printed data frame
title: Getting started with daos
text: Enable automatically capture data frame returned console .last.df global environment. Handy forget assign intermediate result.
code: track_last_df()          # enable  dplyr::starwars |> head(3) .last.df                 # the three-row data frame above  track_last_df(FALSE)     # disable

title: Getting started with daos
text: time2screen() launches interactive Shiny app reviewing time series group group. columns x, y, series, excluded automatically become dropdown filters. Navigate keyboard (← / →) click buttons. Press Esc exit. Requires shiny highcharter.
code: library(shiny) library(highcharter)  # Simple example — one line per group: df <- expand.grid(   year    = 2010:2020,   country = c("DK", "SE", "NO", "FI") ) df$gdp <- rnorm(nrow(df), mean = 300, sd = 30)  time2screen(df, x = year, y = gdp)  # With a series dimension for multiple lines per group: ggplot2::economics_long |>   time2screen(date, value, series = variable)

text: Daniel Madsen. Author, maintainer.

code: @Manual{,   title = {daos: Utility Functions for Statistical Work},   author = {Daniel Madsen},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0}, }

title: Utility Functions for Statistical Work
text: Experimental. package personal collection utility functions grown day--day statistical work — things kept wishing R box, patterns found repeating, new ideas wanted try . Rather letting accumulate loose scripts, packaged properly help Claude Code get solid starting point: documentation, tests, vignette start. Consider living experiment.

title: Utility Functions for Statistical Work
code: pak::pak("dataniel/daos")

what: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
text: Vectorised extraction birth date, age, sex, validity indicators Danish CPR (Civil Person Register) numbers. Returns original data frame requested columns appended.

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
code: cpr_info(   data,   cpr_col,   add = c("bday", "age", "sex", "mod11", "valid"),   ref_date = Sys.Date() )

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
text: data tibble data frame. cpr_col Name CPR column (unquoted). add info types add. Either: unnamed character vector, e.g. c("bday", "age") — uses type names column names. named character vector, e.g. c(birth_date = "bday", years_old = "age") — left-hand side becomes column name, right-hand side type. Default: six types. ref_date Reference date age calculation. Accepts Date object ISO-format string ("YYYY-MM-DD"). Default: Sys.Date().

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
text: original data frame requested columns appended.

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
text: Supported info types (add values): Century detection follows official CPR Register rules based digit 7 two-digit year component. Input tolerance: dashes spaces stripped automatically nine-digit numbers zero-padded left (recovering values lost leading zero Excel). CPR column returned data frame always returned standardised 10-digit format xxxxxxxxxx.

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
code: df <- data.frame(   pnr = c("1111111118", "111111-1118", "111111118"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Default: add all columns cpr_info(df, pnr) #>          pnr       bday age sex mod11 valid #> 1 1111111118 1911-11-11 114   0  TRUE  TRUE #> 2 1111111118 1911-11-11 114   0  TRUE  TRUE #> 3 0111111118 1911-11-01 114   0 FALSE  TRUE  # Custom column names: cpr_info(df, pnr, add = c(birth_date = "bday", years_old = "age")) #>          pnr birth_date years_old #> 1 1111111118 1911-11-11       114 #> 2 1111111118 1911-11-11       114 #> 3 0111111118 1911-11-01       114  # Unnamed — uses type names directly: cpr_info(df, pnr, add = c("bday", "sex")) #>          pnr       bday sex #> 1 1111111118 1911-11-11   0 #> 2 1111111118 1911-11-11   0 #> 3 0111111118 1911-11-01   0

what: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
text: pipeline-friendly validation checkpoint. Emits success alert data frame zero rows, otherwise warns aborts. Optionally appends timestamped entry log file — useful automated pipelines want minimal audit trail without noise.

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
code: expect_empty(   data,   success_msg = "The dataset is empty.",   warn_msg = "The dataset is not empty.",   abort_msg = NULL,   log = NULL )

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
text: data data frame tibble. success_msg Message shown (logged) data empty. Default: "dataset empty.". warn_msg Message shown (logged) data empty abort_msg set. Default: "dataset empty.". abort_msg provided, cli::cli_abort() called message data empty. NULL (default), warning issued instead. log Optional path log file. directory created automatically exist. entry prefixed timestamp symbol (✔, ✖, !).

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
text: data invisibly.

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
code: # Success — no rows data.frame() |> expect_empty() #> ✔ The dataset is empty.  # Warning — unexpected rows dplyr::filter(ggplot2::mpg, cyl < 0) |>   expect_empty(warn_msg = "Negative cylinder counts found") #> ✔ The dataset is empty.  # Abort — treat unexpected rows as a hard error if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ dplyr::filter(dplyr::starwars, height < 0) |>   expect_empty(abort_msg = "Impossible: negative height") } # }  # With logging: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ log_path <- f("log/{nowf()}/checks.log") checker  <- purrr::partial(expect_empty, log = log_path)  dplyr::filter(dplyr::starwars, name == "Harry Potter") |>   checker(success_msg = "No Harry Potter rows") } # }

title: String interpolation shorthand — f
text: short alias glue::glue(). Interpolates R expressions enclosed {} inside string. arguments accepted glue::glue() forwarded.

title: String interpolation shorthand — f
code: f(   ...,   .sep = "",   .envir = parent.frame(),   .open = "{",   .close = "}",   .na = "NA",   .null = character(),   .comment = "#",   .literal = FALSE,   .transformer = identity_transformer,   .trim = TRUE )

title: String interpolation shorthand — f
text: ... [expressions] Unnamed arguments taken expression string(s) format. Multiple inputs concatenated together formatting. Named arguments taken temporary variables available substitution. glue_data(), elements ... override values .x. .sep [character(1): ‘""’] Separator used separate elements. .envir [environment: parent.frame()] Environment evaluate expression . Expressions evaluated left right. .x environment, expressions evaluated environment .envir ignored. NULL passed, equivalent emptyenv(). .open [character(1): ‘\{’] opening delimiter. Doubling full delimiter escapes . .close [character(1): ‘\}’] closing delimiter. Doubling full delimiter escapes . .na [character(1): ‘NA’] Value replace NA values . NULL missing values propagated, NA result cause NA output. Otherwise value replaced value .na. .null [character(1): ‘character()’] Value replace NULL values . character() whole output character(). NULL NULL values dropped (paste0()). Otherwise value replaced value .null. .comment [character(1): ‘#’] Value use comment character. .literal [boolean(1): ‘FALSE’] Whether treat single double quotes, backticks, comments regular characters (vs. syntactic elements), parsing expression string. Setting .literal = TRUE probably makes sense combination custom .transformer, case glue_col(). Regard argument (especially, name) experimental. .transformer [function] function taking two arguments, text envir, text unparsed string inside glue block envir execution environment. .transformer lets modify glue block , , evaluation, allowing create custom glue()-like functions. See vignette("transformers") examples. .trim [logical(1): ‘TRUE’] Whether trim input template trim() .

title: String interpolation shorthand — f
text: character vector interpolated strings.

title: String interpolation shorthand — f
code: f("2 + 2 = {2 + 2}") #> 2 + 2 = 4 name <- "world" f("Hello, {name}!") #> Hello, world!  # Combine with nowf() for timestamped paths: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ f("data/export_{nowf('%Y%m%d')}.parquet") f("log/{nowf()}/0-check.log") } # }

what: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
title: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
text: Given set labelled values target total, determines multipliers (+1, -1, 0) apply value signed sum equals target. Uses meet---middle algorithm efficiency.

title: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
code: find_signs(   df,   label_col,   value_col,   total_label = "total",   positive = NULL,   negative = NULL,   by = NULL,   max_zeros = 2L )

title: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
text: df data frame containing labels values. label_col Column containing item labels (unquoted). value_col Column containing numeric values (unquoted). total_label Label row whose value reconciliation target. Default: "total". positive Character vector labels must receive positive sign. Optional. negative Character vector labels must receive negative sign. Optional. Optional grouping columns (unquoted). supplied, reconciliation performed separately group. max_zeros Maximum number zero coefficients allowed valid solution (.e. many items can excluded). Default: 2L.

title: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
text: tibble label/value structure input, values multiplied resolved signs. Groups unique solution silently dropped (NULL).

title: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
text: useful reconciling accounting line items sign convention unknown — example, finding items set account balances add reported total.

title: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
code: items <- data.frame(   label = c("revenue", "costs", "depreciation", "total"),   value = c(100, 40, 10, 50) ) find_signs(items, label, value, total_label = "total") #> # A tibble: 0 × 0

title: Flag duplicate rows — flag_duplicates
text: Prepends two columns data frame: isdup (logical, TRUE row appears ) dupid (integer group identifier, 0 unique rows). Uses data.table internally speed.

title: Flag duplicate rows — flag_duplicates
code: flag_duplicates(data, ...)

title: Flag duplicate rows — flag_duplicates
text: data data frame tibble. ... Columns used identify duplicates (tidy-select). omitted, columns used.

title: Flag duplicate rows — flag_duplicates
text: tibble isdup dupid prepended.

title: Flag duplicate rows — flag_duplicates
code: # All columns flag_duplicates(ggplot2::mpg) #> # A tibble: 234 × 13 #>    isdup dupid manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy #>    <lgl> <int> <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> <int> <int> #>  1 FALSE     0 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto… f        18    29 #>  2 FALSE     0 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 #>  3 FALSE     0 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 #>  4 FALSE     0 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 #>  5 FALSE     0 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto… f        16    26 #>  6 FALSE     0 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manu… f        18    26 #>  7 FALSE     0 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 #>  8 FALSE     0 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manu… 4        18    26 #>  9 FALSE     0 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 #> 10 FALSE     0 audi         a4 quattro   2    2008     4 manu… 4        20    28 #> # ℹ 224 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: fl <chr>, class <chr>  # Specific columns flag_duplicates(ggplot2::mpg, manufacturer, model, year) #> # A tibble: 234 × 13 #>    isdup dupid manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy #>    <lgl> <int> <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> <int> <int> #>  1 TRUE      1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto… f        18    29 #>  2 TRUE      1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 #>  3 TRUE      2 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 #>  4 TRUE      2 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 #>  5 TRUE      1 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto… f        16    26 #>  6 TRUE      1 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manu… f        18    26 #>  7 TRUE      2 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 #>  8 TRUE      3 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manu… 4        18    26 #>  9 TRUE      3 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 #> 10 TRUE      4 audi         a4 quattro   2    2008     4 manu… 4        20    28 #> # ℹ 224 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: fl <chr>, class <chr>  # Combined with expect_empty() for a validation pipeline: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggplot2::mpg |>   flag_duplicates() |>   dplyr::filter(isdup) |>   expect_empty(warn_msg = "Duplicate rows found") } # }

title: Extended null-coalescing operator — %??%
text: Returns default x NULL, length 0, consists entirely NA values, (character vectors) consists entirely empty strings. Otherwise returns x unchanged.

title: Extended null-coalescing operator — %??%
code: x %??% default

title: Extended null-coalescing operator — %??%
text: x Value test. default Fallback value returned x blank.

title: Extended null-coalescing operator — %??%
text: x non-blank, otherwise default.

title: Extended null-coalescing operator — %??%
text: intuitive rlang::%||% data-cleaning contexts empty strings -NA vectors treated missing.

title: Extended null-coalescing operator — %??%
code: NULL %??% "default" #> [1] "default" NA %??% 0 #> [1] 0 "" %??% "unknown" #> [1] "unknown" c(NA, NA) %??% "missing" #> [1] "missing" 42 %??% 99 #> [1] 42

title: Regex matching with NA preservation — %like%
text: infix operator equivalent grepl(), NA values x remain NA result instead coerced FALSE.

title: Regex matching with NA preservation — %like%
code: x %like% pattern

title: Regex matching with NA preservation — %like%
text: x vector search . pattern regular expression (see regex).

title: Regex matching with NA preservation — %like%
text: logical vector length x.

title: Regex matching with NA preservation — %like%
code: c("a1", "b2", NA, "c") %like% "\\d" #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE    NA FALSE  # Use in dplyr pipelines: if (requireNamespace("dplyr", quietly = TRUE)) {   dplyr::filter(ggplot2::mpg, model %like% "\\d+") } #> # A tibble: 114 × 11 #>    manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy fl    class #>    <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> #>  1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto… f        18    29 p     comp… #>  2 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 p     comp… #>  3 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 p     comp… #>  4 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 p     comp… #>  5 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto… f        16    26 p     comp… #>  6 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manu… f        18    26 p     comp… #>  7 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 p     comp… #>  8 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manu… 4        18    26 p     comp… #>  9 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 p     comp… #> 10 audi         a4 quattro   2    2008     4 manu… 4        20    28 p     comp… #> # ℹ 104 more rows

title: Test whether a value is
text: Returns TRUE x NULL, length 0, contains NA values, (character vectors) contains empty strings. intuitive rlang::is_empty() data-cleaning contexts.

title: Test whether a value is
code: is_blank(x)

title: Test whether a value is
text: x R value.

title: Test whether a value is
text: single TRUE FALSE.

title: Test whether a value is
code: is_blank(NULL)        # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(NA)          # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank("")          # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(c(NA, NA))   # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(0)           # FALSE #> [1] FALSE is_blank("text")      # FALSE #> [1] FALSE  # Compare with rlang::is_empty(): # is_empty("") returns FALSE, is_blank("") returns TRUE

title: Formatted timestamp for now — nowf
text: shorthand format(lubridate::now(), fmt). Useful constructing file names log paths timestamp baked .

title: Formatted timestamp for now — nowf
code: nowf(fmt = "%Y%m%d")

title: Formatted timestamp for now — nowf
text: fmt Format string accepted strftime(). Default "%Y%m%d" (ISO date without separators).

title: Formatted timestamp for now — nowf
text: single character string formatted timestamp.

title: Formatted timestamp for now — nowf
code: nowf()                 # e.g. "20260518" #> [1] "20260520" nowf("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")  # e.g. "20260518_143022" #> [1] "20260520_222250" nowf("%Y%B")           # e.g. "2026May" #> [1] "2026May"  # Typical use in file paths: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ f("log/{nowf()}/0-check_data.log") f("data/export_{nowf('%Y%m%d_%H%M%S')}.parquet") } # }

title: Suppress messages and warnings — quiet
text: Evaluates expression suppressing message() warning() calls. Useful loading packages calling verbose functions.

title: Suppress messages and warnings — quiet
code: quiet(expr)

title: Suppress messages and warnings — quiet
text: expr R expression evaluate silently.

title: Suppress messages and warnings — quiet
text: return value expr (unchanged).

title: Suppress messages and warnings — quiet
code: quiet(message("this message will not appear")) quiet(warning("this warning will not appear"))  # Typical use: load packages without startup text if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ quiet(library(tidyverse)) } # }

what: Read one or more files — read_files
title: Read one or more files — read_files
text: Expands paths using glue::glue() ({0:9} generates ten paths), checks every file exists, reads automatic format detection custom reader, optionally row-binds unpacks result.

title: Read one or more files — read_files
code: read_files(   paths,   names = NULL,   reader = "auto",   out = NULL,   .envir = parent.frame(),   .id = NULL,   .overwrite = FALSE,   .lowercase = TRUE,   ... )

title: Read one or more files — read_files
text: paths character vector file paths. Glue syntax ({}) supported compact range expansion. names Optional names result. Defaults file names without extension. numeric, .id column (= "bind") also numeric. reader "auto" (default) detect format file extension, function \(path, ...) ... use custom reader. Controls returned reading: NULL (default): object directly single file; named list multiple files. "bind": row-bind data frames single tibble. column types differ, warning issued types reconciled readr::type_convert(). source column added .id set. "unpack": assign element named variable .envir. .envir Environment used glue interpolation (= "unpack") target assignment. Default calling frame. .id Name source column added = "bind". NULL (default), source column added. .overwrite FALSE (default), aborts name already exists .envir = "unpack". Set TRUE allow overwrites. .lowercase TRUE (default), column names converted lowercase reading. Set FALSE preserve original casing. ... Additional arguments forwarded reader function.

title: Read one or more files — read_files
text: Depends : NULL: object (single file) named list (multiple files). "bind": single tibble. "unpack": named list, invisibly.

title: Read one or more files — read_files
text: Supported formats (reader = "auto"): Note CSV: uses readr::read_csv2() expects semicolon-separated values comma decimal mark (Danish/European convention). comma-separated files, pass custom reader: reader = readr::read_csv. Packages non-base formats (arrow, haven, readxl, jsonlite, yaml) must installed separately.

title: Read one or more files — read_files
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Single file (returns object directly): df <- read_files("data/results.parquet")  # Multiple files with glue expansion: lst <- read_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet", names = 0:9)  # Custom reader: lst <- read_files(   "data/dat{0:9}.parquet",   reader = \(x) arrow::read_parquet(x, col_select = 1:5) )  # Read and bind into one tibble: df <- read_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet", names = 0:9, out = "bind")  # Read and unpack into individual variables (dat0, dat1, ...): read_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet", names = paste0("dat", 0:9), out = "unpack") summon("^dat\\d+$") } # }

what: Read a Greenlandic TA file — read_ta
title: Read a Greenlandic TA file — read_ta
text: Reads Greenlandic TA file. Supports current prices (L), constant prices (F), prior-year prices (D) file types.

title: Read a Greenlandic TA file — read_ta
code: read_ta(ta)

title: Read a Greenlandic TA file — read_ta
text: ta Path TA file.

title: Read a Greenlandic TA file — read_ta
text: tibble columns described .

title: Read a Greenlandic TA file — read_ta
text: Column positions (0-based byte offsets): Columns nrnr, trans, brch, afstm, fval read character; others double.

title: Read a Greenlandic TA file — read_ta
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ df <- read_ta("ta.file") } # }

what: Show object sizes in an environment — size_env
title: Show object sizes in an environment — size_env
text: Lists objects environment sorted size (descending), raw byte counts human-readable representations.

title: Show object sizes in an environment — size_env
code: size_env(.envir = parent.frame(), n = NULL)

title: Show object sizes in an environment — size_env
text: .envir environment inspect. Default calling environment. n Optional integer. supplied, n largest objects returned.

title: Show object sizes in an environment — size_env
text: tibble columns name (character), size (numeric, bytes), pretty (fs_bytes, human-readable). Returns NULL invisibly environment empty.

title: Show object sizes in an environment — size_env
code: x <- 1:1e6 y <- letters size_env()       # all objects #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   name     size      pretty #>   <chr>   <dbl> <fs::bytes> #> 1 x     4000048       3.81M #> 2 y        1712       1.67K size_env(n = 1)  # only the largest #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   name     size      pretty #>   <chr>   <dbl> <fs::bytes> #> 1 x     4000048       3.81M

what: Split a data frame into a named list by grouping columns — split_by
title: Split a data frame into a named list by grouping columns — split_by
text: Groups data frame splits , automatically naming element resulting list unique group key values. Unlike bare dplyr::group_split(), returned list always named.

title: Split a data frame into a named list by grouping columns — split_by
code: split_by(data, ..., .sep = "_")

title: Split a data frame into a named list by grouping columns — split_by
text: data data frame tibble. ... Grouping columns (unquoted, tidy-select). .sep Separator used concatenate multiple group key values single list name. Default: "_".

title: Split a data frame into a named list by grouping columns — split_by
text: named list tibbles.

title: Split a data frame into a named list by grouping columns — split_by

what: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
text: Searches environment objects whose names match regular expression returns named list. Useful collecting family similarly-named objects (e.g. dat0 dat9) unpacking list unpack_files().

title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
code: summon(pattern, .envir = parent.frame())

title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
text: pattern single regular expression string. .envir environment search. Default calling environment.

title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
text: named list matching objects.

title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
code: dat1 <- data.frame(x = 1) dat2 <- data.frame(x = 2) dat3 <- data.frame(x = 3) summon("^dat\\d+$") #> $dat1 #>   x #> 1 1 #>  #> $dat2 #>   x #> 1 2 #>  #> $dat3 #>   x #> 1 3 #>

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
text: Launches Shiny application visually reviewing time-series data group group. columns x, y, series, excluded become grouping dimensions dropdown selectors. Navigate groups arrow keys. Press Space flag current combination, R reset zoom, Q quit.

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
code: time2screen(   data,   x,   y,   series = NULL,   .exclude = NULL,   .title = NULL,   .y_min = NULL,   .y_max = NULL )

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
text: data data frame containing minimum x-axis column, y-axis column, least one grouping column. x Time (x-axis) variable. Accepts Date, POSIXct, numeric years, value coercible date. (Unquoted.) y Numeric measurement (y-axis) variable. (Unquoted.) series Optional variable plotting multiple lines per group (e.g. category). (Unquoted.) .exclude Columns exclude becoming grouping dropdowns. (Unquoted, tidy-select.) .title Optional title string shown app header downloaded figures. group combination (· b · c) always shown separately affected. .y_min Optional numeric. Pre-fills Y min input, fixing lower bound y-axis globally across groups. Leave NULL automatic scaling. Set 0 replicate old "start zero" behaviour. .y_max Optional numeric. Pre-fills Y max input, fixing upper bound y-axis globally across groups. Leave NULL automatic scaling.

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
text: data frame flagged group combinations (key columns ), NULL nothing flagged. Returned invisibly app exits.

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
text: Requires shiny highcharter packages.

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(dplyr)  # Simple example with economics dataset: flagged <- ggplot2::economics_long |>   time2screen(date, value, series = variable)  # With a grouping column: df <- data.frame(   year    = rep(2010:2020, 3),   country = rep(c("DK", "SE", "NO"), each = 11),   gdp     = rnorm(33, 300, 20) ) flagged <- time2screen(df, x = year, y = gdp) } # }

what: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
text: Installs task callback automatically saves data frame returned console named variable global environment. Intermediate expressions (1 + 1, plots, etc.) ignored — top-level data frame returns captured.

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
code: track_last_df(on = TRUE, name = ".last.df")

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
text: TRUE enable tracking (default), FALSE disable. name Name variable written .GlobalEnv. Default: ".last.df".

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
text: TRUE invisibly.

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
text: Calling track_last_df() replaces existing callback, preventing duplicates.

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ track_last_df()          # enable dplyr::starwars |> head() .last.df                 # the last printed data frame  track_last_df(FALSE)     # disable } # }

what: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
text: Shows column types one data frames side--side. Useful diagnosing type mismatches join binding read_files().

title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
code: view_types(..., diff = FALSE, focus = NULL)

title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
text: ... Data frames compare (unquoted). Names inferred expressions passed. diff Logical. TRUE, columns types differ across datasets shown. Default: FALSE. focus named length-1 character vector, e.g. c(year = "int"). supplied, named column shown, datasets type match expected type. Returns empty tibble (0 rows) types match — suitable use tests.

title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
text: tibble column column one column per input dataset showing pillar::type_sum() type string.

title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
code: df1 <- data.frame(x = 1L, y = "a") df2 <- data.frame(x = 1.0, y = "b")  view_types(df1, df2) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   column df1   df2   #>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> #> 1 x      int   dbl   #> 2 y      chr   chr   view_types(df1, df2, diff = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   column df1   df2   #>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> #> 1 x      int   dbl   view_types(df1, df2, focus = c(x = "int")) #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>   column df2   #>   <chr>  <chr> #> 1 x      dbl
